course_description:
This course allows students to undertake an internship and related work-integrated learning.

This course consists of an industry internship in the field of writing, editing, and publishing, or communication. Students will undertake 80-100 hours of work-integrated learning, "solve" a problem for their provider, deliver a 5 minute presentation about their work experience, and write a reflective essay.

prerequisites: You'll need to complete the following courses before enrolling in this one:
incompatible: You can't enrol in this course if you've already completed the following:

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable
.
course_aims: To enable students to undertake and reflect on a work-integrated learning experience.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically and reflexively analyse workplace tasks and apply effective problem-solving in real-world contexts
Communicate workplace insights confidently and respond effectively to questions and feedback
Evaluate peers' work and develop feedback skills aligned with industry practice
Identify and reflect on learning gained through the internship and articulate how it can be applied to future professional contexts.

assessment_summary.title: Case Study: Identify a Problem in the Workplace
assessment_summary.due_date: 13/04/2026 4:00 pm; Submit via PeerMark link through Blackboard to enable Peer Review

assessment_details.title: Case Study: Identify a Problem in the Workplace
assessment_details.due_date: 13/04/2026 4:00 pm Submit via PeerMark link through Blackboard to enable Peer Review
assessment_details.other_conditions: Peer assessment factor, Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assessment asks students to analyse a real problem they encountered and will try to resolve during their internship. Skills include being able to identify and critically analyse problems, solution-focused thinking, turning problems into opportunities, analysing strengths and weaknesses of workplace issues, ability to pitch an idea to persuade colleagues, resource evaluation skills, writing skills, ability to work within a brief. Task Description: Identify a problem or opportunity in the workplace where you are undertaking your internship. It could be a system that could be streamlined, a lack of documentation in some aspect of work flow, or an idea for software or equipment that would make things easier for management or employees. Write up a 900-1100 word proposal: Paragraph 1: a concise statement of the problem or opportunity Paragraphs 2 & 3: your ideas for resolving it - how you propose to do so Paragraph 4: any resourcing issues applicable to your solution Feel free to talk through with your internship provider, but please make sure they know this is a hypothetical only. If they want you to implement the solution, they will let you know. Pass/Fail hurdle for this assessment only: You must achieve all of the following tasks: Submit your case study by the due date via the PeerMark/Turnitin Link Review 2 x peer case studies by the Week 8 workshop on PeerMark Attend the Week 8 workshop to respond, in-person, to peer feedback and questions. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
TurnItIn Receipts:
This assignment will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Reflective Essay
assessment_details.other_conditions: Student specific, Work integrated learning. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: This assignment assesses your ability to reflect on experiences and draw learning outcomes from those experiences. It helps you apply learning outcomes to future scenarios. Students are required to submit a reflective essay of 2100–2300 words (excluding references, appendices or work-related extracts) based on their experiences and observations during their internship. The essay is an opportunity to critically evaluate your professional development, reflect on workplace tasks, and connect your experience to industry practices and emerging technologies. The essay should address 4 out of 5 of the following questions, with each response approximately 500–600 words : How have your attitudes, perspectives, or approaches to work changed as a result of the internship? Reflect on one or two significant tasks you completed (e.g., media releases, blog posts, editorial reports, biographies, research). What challenges did you encounter, what successes did you achieve, and what lessons did you learn? Which industry standards, editorial guidelines, or professional practices did you find most valuable or surprising? How did you navigate your organisation’s approach to AI, including any restrictions or encouragement regarding GenAI use? Did this experience influence your attitude toward or use of AI? How has the internship influenced your career aspirations or professional identity? You must include screenshots or extracts (as appendices) of work produced for the internship at least once (e.g., media releases, blog posts, editorial reports, biographies, research, letters, emails, meeting minutes), and directly discuss/analyse them in your reflection. As a screenshot or appendix, these do not count toward your word count total. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools Read full task description Hide full task description

learning_resources.library_resources: Library resources are available on the UQ Library website .

learning_activities.topic: Week 2: Workshop 1 During this workshop we'll introduce ourselves and clarify the expectations of both you and your internship company. We'll also have an assessment overview so you can start planning early - reflections rely on weekly journalling. Learning outcomes: L01, L02

Week 5: Workshop 2 During this workshop we'll share our internship experiences to date, meet previous interns, learn to resolve any issues, and discuss the upcoming case study assessment including how to use PeerMark to review your peers. Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
Week 8: Workshop 3 Peer Review session as part of your craft analysis assessment; employability discussion Learning outcomes: L02
Week 11: Workshop 4 We'll be wrapping up with a workshop about how to write a reflection essay. Learning outcomes: L01, L02
Week 1 to Week 13: 80-100 hours of work-integrated learning Working as confirmed with internship provider during semester 1 Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
